title: Binary Search — Dry Run & Recall
oneSentence: Trace Binary Search, Search in Rotated Sorted Array, and Median of Two Sorted Arrays.

binarySearch.title: Binary Search
binarySearch.description: Find target in a sorted array using standard halving bounds.
binarySearch.story: Opening a dictionary to the middle, checking if word is before or after, and pruning half the pages.
code: public static int solve(int[] nums, int target) {

steps.desc: Init: left pointer at index 0 (val -1), right pointer at index 5 (val 12).
Loop check: left <= right (0 <= 5) is true. Compute mid = 0 + (5 - 0) / 2 = index 2 (val 3).
Compare nums[mid] (3) with target (9). Since 3 < 9, target must be in the right half. Move left to mid + 1.
left updated to index 3 (val 5).
Loop check: left <= right (3 <= 5) is true. Compute mid = 3 + (5 - 3) / 2 = index 4 (val 9).
Compare nums[mid] (9) with target (9). They match! Return mid index 4.

quiz.q: Why is 'left + (right - left) / 2' preferred over '(left + right) / 2'?
options: It avoids integer overflow in systems where left + right can exceed the maximum integer size.
It automatically rounds up instead of down.
It runs in O(1) time while the other runs in O(log N) time.
quiz.explain: If left and right are very large, left + right can overflow past 2^31 - 1, producing a negative number. Writing it as left + (right - left) / 2 avoids this overflow.

searchInRotatedSortedArray.title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
searchInRotatedSortedArray.description: Find a target in a sorted array that has been rotated.
searchInRotatedSortedArray.story: Shuffled dictionary: one half is always sorted. Determine which half is sorted, check if target lies within its bounds, and prune.
code: public static int solve(int[] nums, int target) {

steps.desc: Init: left at index 0 (val 4), right at index 6 (val 2).
Loop check: 0 <= 6. Compute mid = 0 + (6-0)/2 = index 3 (val 7).
Check sorted half: nums[left] (4) <= nums[mid] (7) is true. Left half [4, 5, 6, 7] is sorted.
Is target (0) in sorted left half bounds [4...7]? (0 >= 4 && 0 < 7) is false. Target must be in right half. Move left to mid + 1.
left updated to index 4 (val 0).
Loop check: 4 <= 6. Compute mid = 4 + (6-4)/2 = index 5 (val 1).
Check sorted half: nums[left] (0) <= nums[mid] (1) is true. Left half [0, 1] is sorted.
Is target (0) in sorted left half bounds [0...1]? (0 >= 0 && 0 < 1) is true! Target is in this range. Move right to mid - 1.
right updated to index 4 (val 0).
Loop check: 4 <= 4. Compute mid = 4 + (4-4)/2 = index 4 (val 0).
nums[mid] (0) == target (0) is true! Match found. Return index 4.

quiz.q: How do we identify which half of the rotated array is sorted at any step?
options: Compare nums[left] with nums[mid]. If nums[left] <= nums[mid], the left half is sorted. Otherwise, the right half is sorted.
Compare nums[mid] with nums[right]. If nums[mid] <= nums[right], the left half is sorted.
The array rotation direction tells us which half is sorted.
quiz.explain: Because the array has only one rotation point, at least one half of the array must be sorted. If nums[left] <= nums[mid], the rotation point is not in the left half, so the left half is sorted.

medianOfTwoSortedArrays.title: Median of Two Sorted Arrays
medianOfTwoSortedArrays.description: Find the median of two sorted arrays in O(log(min(M, N))) time.
medianOfTwoSortedArrays.story: Comparing partitions of two assembly lines: ensure everything on the left is <= everything on the right.
code: public static double solve(int[] nums1, int[] nums2) {

steps.desc: Init: Ensure nums1 (len 5) is shorter than nums2 (len 6). Set binary search range [0, 5] on nums1.
Partition step: i = mid of nums1 = 2. j = (5+6+1)/2 - 2 = 4. Boundary check values: maxLeft1=nums1[1]=3, minRight1=nums1[2]=8. maxLeft2=nums2[3]=19, minRight2=nums2[4]=21.

Check validity: maxLeft1 <= minRight2 (3 <= 21) is true, but maxLeft2 <= minRight1 (19 <= 8) is false! The partition in nums1 is too far left (we need larger elements from nums1 on the left side). Move left pointer to i + 1.
left pointer updated to index 3.
Partition step: i = 4. j = (5+6+1)/2 - 4 = 2. Boundary checks: maxLeft1=nums1[3]=9, minRight1=nums1[4]=15. maxLeft2=nums2[1]=11, minRight2=nums2[2]=18.

Check validity: maxLeft1 <= minRight2 (9 <= 18) is true, and maxLeft2 <= minRight1 (11 <= 15) is true! Valid partition found! Compute median: since total length is odd (5+6 = 11), return max(maxLeft1, maxLeft2) = max(9, 11) = 11.0.

quiz.q: Why must we run the binary search on the shorter array in Median of Two Sorted Arrays?
options: To ensure the binary search takes O(log(min(M, N))) time and to prevent index out of bounds when calculating j.
Because the longer array is not guaranteed to be sorted.
It is a random choice; searching the longer array is equally efficient and safe.
quiz.explain: Searching the shorter array guarantees that partition index j = (m + n + 1) / 2 - i is always a valid index in the longer array, and it minimizes the search space range, yielding O(log(min(M,N))) time complexity.
